questions.prompt: Complete: "This weekend Yosra ___ buy a souvenir at Camden Market."
questions.explanation: Yosra is one person (she), so the plan is "is going to" + the base verb: is going to buy. "Are going to" is for we/you/they, "going to" drops the verb be, and "is going" forgets the word "to".

questions.prompt: Complete: "Skander and his cousins ___ take the Tube to Oxford Street."
questions.explanation: "Skander and his cousins" are several people (they), so we use "are going to" + base verb: are going to take. "Am going to" is only for I, "is going to" is for one person, and "going to" is missing the verb be.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The River Thames is ___ than the small stream near our house."
questions.explanation: "Wide" is a short adjective, so its comparative adds -r: wider than. "Wide" alone is not a comparison, "more wide" marks it twice, and "widest" is the superlative, not a comparison of two things.

questions.prompt: Complete: "In my opinion, the British Museum is ___ than a shopping street."
questions.explanation: "Interesting" is a long adjective (more than two syllables), so its comparative is "more interesting than". "Interestinger" does not exist, "interesting" alone is not a comparison, and "most interesting" is a superlative.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Skander thinks the London Eye is ___ wheel in the whole city."
questions.explanation: One wheel above all the others in the city is a superlative: the biggest (short adjective, double the -g and add -est). "The bigger" is a comparative, "the most big" is wrong for a short adjective, and "biggest" forgets "the".

questions.prompt: Complete this suggestion: "___ go to Hyde Park by bus — it is cheaper than a taxi."
questions.explanation: "Let's" + the base verb makes a suggestion: Let's go. "How about" needs -ing (How about going), "Let's to" adds an extra "to", and "Why don't" needs a subject (Why don't we go).
